summary: Gruber examines the shared characteristics of his 2004 Mac apps of the year — Interarchy, Affrus, SpamSieve, and BBEdit — and finds three common traits: excellent documentation, strong scripting/extensibility, and tabbed interfaces. He argues that good documentation remains undervalued, that AppleScript support builds loyal user communities even when most users don't write scripts themselves, and that tabbed interfaces are proliferating rapidly but inconsistently across Mac apps. He calls on Apple to standardize tab widgets and interface guidelines before implementations diverge too far. The post closes with the pointed observation that none of these standout apps came from Apple itself.
key_points: All the top apps have thorough, well-written documentation — wikis alone are insufficient for documenting complex software
Scripting and extensibility let power users create custom workflows, and those users share scripts that build community around an app
Tabbed interfaces spread rapidly from browsers to other app categories, but implementations vary wildly in appearance and behavior
Apple should provide standardized tab widgets and guidelines before the inconsistency becomes permanent
None of the year's best Mac apps were made by Apple
money_quotes: Making an app scriptable isn't just a check-off feature — it's about allowing users to check-off their own individual customized features.
Wikis seldom produce complete documentation; they're a good way to aggregate user-contributed tips and tricks, but they're a poor way to completely document a complex app.
Users who do write scripts are almost always willing to share, posting scripts to forums, mailings lists, and weblogs. It helps build a loyal community of users around an app.
Only Apple can prevent this from turning into a permanent free-for-all of disparate and inconsistent appearances and behaviors.
One last thing worth noting about these apps, by the way: none of them came from Apple.
I've written scripts for my own use with these apps that I would never ask for as built-in functions in the apps themselves, but which save me significant amounts of time.
key_insight: The best Mac software distinguishes itself not through flashy features but through the fundamentals — documentation, scriptability, and thoughtful UI conventions — and notably, Apple itself isn't leading by example.
